Core Discipline number two is to be able to wire frame a website. This is apart from creating the Storybrand OnePage, this is probably the next thing that you want to do for a client or for yourself. One of the main reasons is it really helps you get all the components of your message onto yet again OnePage so that customers can understand and respond. A great website can be worth hundreds of thousands or even millions and millions of dollars to a client. We've got to nail it down and get it right. What happens to most people when they create a website is they really are so close to their product or service that they speak inside language. They say things that nobody really understands. They understand it but the client doesn't understand it. So as a Storybrand copywriter, you want to be able to look at a website and immediately say that's a waste of words. This should be here. You're going to want to move this down. There are too many words in this paragraph. You want to be able to be kind of a marketing ninja and be able to diagnose what's wrong with a website. We're going to teach you to do that in this module. Wireframing a website can be a very creative experience. Of course, you're going to have to use your judgment. It's largely subjective. But there are essentially 10 components that we've proven really work and there are going to be 10 weapons that you're going to be able to use, 10 tools that you're going to be able to use to create a really great website for your customer. Now remember, we're just talking about wireframing the website. So you won't actually be designing it unless you are a designer. You'll actually just be diagramming it so that the company or you can actually take it to a designer and they'll know exactly what to say. Your designer is going to love you because you're going to do so much of the work for them. The very first component is the header. Now you're going to start with the header. This is the top of the website. And the main thing that you want the header to do is to pass the grunt test. And what do I mean by pass the grunt test? I mean, if you took that laptop and you gave that website to a caveman to look at for five seconds. Could the caveman grunt an answer to three critical questions? The first is what do you offer? How will it make my life better? And then what do I need to do to buy it? If you are wireframing a website, the most important thing to remember is that the header, the very first impression, what is above the fold, the first thing that the web browser looks at, answer these three questions. And it needs to do it in very bold text, without a lot of clutter, without a lot of buttons. Because if you can't answer these three questions when you go to a website, you're probably never going to buy anything from them. So remember that when you create a website, when you wireframe a website, the header must pass the grunt test. Another thing I want you to remember as you're wireframing a website is that people don't read websites anymore. They scan them. So if you have a lot of text, but there's no bold print or no icons, or it's not visually interesting, people aren't going to engage that website. They don't read websites anymore. They scan them. You want to appeal to people's squirrel brains. And one of the ways that you appeal to the squirrel brain, if you will, people just scanning information, is to create great images. Think about the images on this website. They are worth a thousand words. So in the header, a smiley, happy person enjoying your product, along with a very clear statement about what the customer is going to get if they do business with you, that's the most important thing. Recently, I had an amazing experience. My family was coming in for Thanksgiving for the holidays. There were 14 people coming to the house. Six or seven of them were guys. And I needed to find something to do to occupy these guys. My wife Betsy said, Don, I want those boys out of the house. Either grab a couple of footballs or something, but make sure they're out of the house. And so I had this crazy idea. I was going to rent laser tag guns. You know those laser tag guns that were so popular? Well, they're really cool now, and they're very, very technologically advanced. And you can rent these guns and have wars in your backyard. And so I just Googled laser tag rental. And I came across a website called Laser Tag Source. And what I loved about this website is right away, it said, We rent and ship laser tag adventures coast to coast. It passes the grunt test. What do you offer? How's it going to make my life better? What do I need to do to buy it? You can see that I can easily make a reservation. They've also broken things down into a three step plan. I really couldn't believe that they got this website so great. As I scroll down the page, I saw pictures of people having fun. And I knew this was the place I was going to rent laser tag guns from. Within 120 seconds, I had my credit card out and I was typing in my number and I spent about $400 renting 10 high end laser guns. I got them a few days later. We had a great Thanksgiving. Everybody said it was magical. It was a wonderful thing. What was amazing though is when I got this box of laser tag guns, I opened the box and there was a handwritten letter inside from their marketing director. And it said, Dear Mr. Miller, we couldn't believe when we saw your order. We're huge fans. We bought your online course and it's really helped us create an efficient website. No wonder it was such a great website. They were one of our customers and they're growing and they're doing great. These are the kind of websites you want to create for your clients or for your business. Websites so that when people go to it, they have their credit card in their hand and they're placing an order within two minutes. Great job laser tag source. Look at this website from CarMax. I talked about CarMax and one of the other modules, but look how this passes the grunt test. Experience clear and simple car buying and selling. How are they going to make my life better? I'm going to car, but I'm also not going to have to deal with the hassle of dealing with a used car dealer. It's going to be clear, easy, authentic and simple. And of course, what do they want me to do? They either choose between shop for a car or sell a car. Now this is an $8.75 billion brand. But look how simple and easy this header is. Very easy to understand, very easy to do business with this company. Here's a company that we've worked with called Equify LLC. They're a company out of Fort Worth. They had a very complicated, difficult website to understand. They sell heavy equipment at auctions, but they also will loan you money based on the value of your heavy equipment. They'll loan you money based on the value of your real estate. But of course, in order to do that, they have to have appraisal services. Then they have to have risk assessment services, so they review all your contracts to make sure they're not liable. If they do lend you money, it's a complicated business. We had to take this very complicated business and this really confusing website and make it simple. And so we chose this, five ways we make your company stronger, and then we listed the five ways. It's clear, it's easy, it passes the grunt test. What do you do? You make my company stronger in these five ways. What do I get out of it? I get, of course, a stronger, more healthy company, and I know how to do business with you. I contact a representative. They had a very confusing website, but now it's really clear. This is another client. His name is Kyle Schultz. He has a photo school, an online photo school, where you can watch an online course that he provides. What do you get out of it? We help parents take better pictures. And then all of the calls to action are very, very clear. This is an example of a great header. I think in Kyle's last product launch, he made $103,000. That's a lot of money. Why? Because he got clear. And look at our website. Clarify your message so people engage. This is Storybrand's website. A live workshop is what it is to help you connect with customers, revolutionize your marketing, and grow your company. That's three things that you get out of it, but it answers the three questions. What do you offer? How will it make my life better? And what do I need to do to buy it? These are several examples of great headers. And the first thing you want to be able to do is create a great header of your own. Another section that you can create on a website, we just call the value proposition. It's where you spell out in bullet points or icons exactly what value the customer is getting by doing business with you. Now, remember people scan websites. They don't read them. So notice this website. This website represents a company that sells a sous vide cooker. It's a kitchen instrument that allows you to cook meat at a very low temperature over a long period of time. So what is the value proposition? Precise control, visual doneness, and notifications. Now notice there are some sentences under each of those bold prints, but this allows me to scroll down a website and scan it rather than read it. If I want to read it, I can slow down and read it, but really I'm looking at these icons in this bold print and I'm scanning the website. Let's go back to laser tag source. What is their value proposition? Affordable pricing, your terms, and of course, custom orders. All laid out, very simply, easy to understand. The customers they're scanning this website is getting a feeling, hey, this is a really good deal. For Schultz Photo School, you have a really creative way of showing the value proposition. It's just a before and after picture. There's a parent's picture of their kid before they took the photo school, and here's what the picture looks like after. That's a great way, before and after pictures, a great way to explain value. And then look at our website, storybrand.com. What do you get out of it? You create a buzz, you learn a language that sells, and you become a marketing master, and of course, register now. Always that direct call to action popping back up. This is what the value proposition looks like. It would be a second, third, doesn't matter, but we want to get it up toward the top of the website after the header, explain the value that somebody will get by doing business with you.
